In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import pyreadr
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

from darts import TimeSeries
from darts.datasets import AirPassengersDataset
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score, mean_absolute_error

Get era5 data and keep only 10 stations in Finland with the most ice data¶

In [2]:
# get era5 data

df = pyreadr.read_r('../Data/era5_met.rds')[None]
df['date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['date'])
df['year'] = df['date'].dt.year
df['month'] = df['date'].dt.month

In [4]:
lakes = pyreadr.read_r('../Data/ltbl_ice.rds')[None]
fs_lakes = set(lakes.loc[lakes['lon_wgs84'] > 0, 'station_id'].unique())
fin_lakes = set(lakes.loc[lakes['country'] == 'FI', 'station_id'].unique())
In [5]:
df = df.loc[df['station_id'].isin(fin_lakes), :]

Merge with ice on ice off data (select n=10 stations with the most ice records)¶

In [6]:
ice = pyreadr.read_r('../Data/data_ip.rds')[None]

ice['ice_on'] = pd.to_datetime(ice['ice_on'])
ice['ice_off'] = pd.to_datetime(ice['ice_off'])
ice['year'] = ice['year'].astype(int)

# keep fin_lakes
ice = ice.loc[ice['station_id'].isin(fin_lakes), :]

# keep where both not null
ice = ice[ice[['ice_on', 'ice_off']].notnull().all(axis=1)]

# cut to same dates as in era and subset to same stations
ice = ice.loc[ice['ice_on'] >= df['date'].min(), :]
ice = ice.loc[ice['ice_off'] <= df['date'].max(), :]

# get top N stations with most records 
topN = (ice.groupby(['station_id', 'year'])
            .size()
            .groupby(level=0)
            .size()
            .sort_values(ascending=False)
            .head(10)).index.to_list()

ice = ice[ice['station_id'].isin(topN)]

Pad ice-on & ice-off time series with nans and fill naively¶

In [8]:
# pad with nans ice-on & ice-off time series for each station

def expand_ice_presence(group):
    min_date = min(df['date'])
    max_date = max(df['date'])
    
    # Sort by year
    group = group.sort_values('year')
    group = group.reset_index(drop=True)

    ice_range = pd.date_range(start=min_date, end=max_date, freq='D')

    # init
    i, row = group.iterrows().__next__()
    ice_df = pd.DataFrame({'date': ice_range, 'station_id': row['station_id'], 'ice_present': np.nan})
    print(f"working on station_id {row['station_id']}")

    # go through station records
    for i, row in group.iterrows():
        year = row['year']
        ice_on = pd.to_datetime(row['ice_on'])
        ice_off = pd.to_datetime(row['ice_off'])

        ice_df.loc[(ice_df['date'] >= ice_on) & (ice_df['date'] < ice_off), 'ice_present'] = 1
        
        # If next year exists and is consecutive, fill with 0
        if i < len(group) - 1 and group.iloc[i+1]['year'] == year + 1:
            # print(f"working on year {year}")
            next_ice_on = pd.to_datetime(group.iloc[i+1]['ice_on'])
            ice_df.loc[(ice_df['date'] >= ice_off) & (ice_df['date'] < next_ice_on), 'ice_present'] = 0
            
    return ice_df

# Apply the function to each station group
# ice.loc[(ice['station_id'] == '624_1') & (ice['year'] > 1980) & (ice['year'] < 1988), :].groupby('station_id').apply(expand_ice_presence).reset_index(drop=True)

df_expanded = ice.groupby('station_id').apply(expand_ice_presence).reset_index(drop=True)

In [11]:
merged_ice_era = merged_ice_era.set_index('date')
In [12]:
## Make into Darts time series

# Create a dictionary to store TimeSeries objects for each station
ts_dict = {}

# Loop through each station
for station in merged_ice_era['station_id'].unique():
    # Filter data for the current station
    station_data = merged_ice_era[merged_ice_era['station_id'] == station]
    
    #ts_tair = TimeSeries.from_dataframe(station_data, 'tair', freq='MS')
    ts_tair = TimeSeries.from_times_and_values(times=station_data.index, values=station_data['tair'], freq='D')
    ts_tp   = TimeSeries.from_times_and_values(times=station_data.index, values=station_data['tp'], freq='D')
    ts_sf   = TimeSeries.from_times_and_values(times=station_data.index, values=station_data['sf'], freq='D')
    ts_ip   = TimeSeries.from_times_and_values(times=station_data.index, values=station_data['ice_present'], freq='D')
    
    # Store both TimeSeries in the dictionary
    ts_dict[station] = {'tair': ts_tair, 'tp': ts_tp, 'sf': ts_sf, 'ip': ts_ip}
In [13]:
def normalize_series(ts):
    """Normalize a TimeSeries to range [0, 1]"""
    values = ts.values()
    min_val = np.min(values)
    max_val = np.max(values)
    normalized_values = (values - min_val) / (max_val - min_val)
    return TimeSeries.from_times_and_values(ts.time_index, normalized_values)

# Assuming ts_dict is your dictionary of TimeSeries objects

normalized_ts_dict = {}

for station_id, series in ts_dict.items():
    normalized_ts_dict[station_id] = {
        'tair': normalize_series(series['tair']),
        'tp': normalize_series(series['tp']),
        'sf': normalize_series(series['sf']),
        'ip': series['ip']  # 0/1 don't normalize
    }
In [14]:
# normalized_ts_dict['723_1']['ip'].plot()
In [15]:
def visualize_station(station_id, ts_dict):
    if station_id not in ts_dict:
        print(f"Station ID {station_id} not found in the dataset.")
        return

    ts = ts_dict[station_id]

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(12, 6), sharex=True)
    fig.suptitle(f"Time Series for Station ID: {station_id}", fontsize=16)

    variables = ['tair', 'tp', 'sf', 'ip']
    colors = ['blue', 'green', 'red', 'purple']

    for ax, var, color in zip(axes, variables, colors):
        df = ts[var].pd_dataframe()

        # Plot the data
        ax.plot(df.index, df.iloc[:, 0], color=color, label=var)
        
        # Highlight NaN values
        nan_mask = df.iloc[:, 0].isna()
        ax.scatter(df.index[nan_mask], [ax.get_ylim()[0]] * nan_mask.sum(), 
                   color='orange', marker='x', s=1, label='NaN')

        ax.set_ylabel(var)
        ax.legend(loc='upper left')
        ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)

    plt.xlabel('Date')
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

visualize_station('621_1', ts_dict)
In [16]:
# naive fill

def fill_nans(series: TimeSeries) -> TimeSeries:
    """Fill NaNs in a time series using forward fill and backward fill."""
    df = series.pd_dataframe()
    # Forward fill, then backward fill for any remaining NaNs at the start
    df_filled = df.ffill().bfill() 
    return TimeSeries.from_dataframe(df_filled)

for station_id, series in normalized_ts_dict.items():
    normalized_ts_dict[station_id]['ip'] = fill_nans(series['ip'])  # 0/1 don't normalize
In [17]:
visualize_station('621_1', normalized_ts_dict)

Darts models¶

We want to select Darts models that are

  • multivariate
  • support multiple timeseries (by station_id)
  • support future covariates (predicted era data)
  • support static covariates (lake properities)

Univariate baseline¶

Let's run a simple univariate Prophet model on temperature

In [18]:
df_single = pd.DataFrame(
    {var : ts_dict[df['station_id'].iloc[0]][var].pd_dataframe()['0'].values
        for var in ['tair', 'tp', 'sf', 'ip']}, 
    index=ts_dict[df['station_id'].iloc[0]]['tair'].pd_dataframe().index)

df_single

In [20]:
from darts import TimeSeries
from darts.models import Prophet
from darts.metrics import mape
import pandas as pd


# Create a TimeSeries object
series = TimeSeries.from_dataframe(df_single.reset_index(),
                                   time_col='date', value_cols=['tair'])

# Split the data into train and test sets
train, test = series.split_before(pd.Timestamp('2010-01-01'))

# Create and train the Prophet model
model = Prophet()
model.fit(train)

# Perform backtesting
backtest = model.historical_forecasts(train, 
                                      start=pd.Timestamp('2000-01-01'),
                                      forecast_horizon=100,
                                      stride=100,
                                      retrain=True,
                                      verbose=True)

mape_scores = mape(actual_series=train, pred_series=backtest, intersect=True)

Multivariate¶

In [24]:
covar_series = TimeSeries.from_dataframe(df_single.reset_index(),
                                         time_col='date', value_cols=['tp', 'sf'])

train_covar, test_covar = covar_series.split_before(pd.Timestamp('2010-01-01'))
In [25]:
from darts.models import XGBModel

# Create and train the XGBModel
model_xgb = XGBModel(lags=50, 
                     lags_past_covariates=50)

model_xgb.fit(train, past_covariates=train_covar)
Out[25]:
XGBModel(lags=50, lags_past_covariates=50, lags_future_covariates=None, output_chunk_length=1, output_chunk_shift=0, add_encoders=None, likelihood=None, quantiles=None, random_state=None, multi_models=True, use_static_covariates=True)
In [26]:
# Perform backtesting
backtest_xgb = model_xgb.historical_forecasts(train,
                                              past_covariates=train_covar,
                                              start=pd.Timestamp('2000-01-01'),
                                              forecast_horizon=10,
                                              stride=10,
                                              retrain=True,
                                              verbose=True)

`enable_optimization=True` is ignored because `retrain` is not `False` or `0`. To hide this warning, set `show_warnings=False` or `enable_optimization=False`.
`enable_optimization=True` is ignored because `forecast_horizon > model.output_chunk_length`. To hide this warning, set `show_warnings=False` or `enable_optimization=False`.

historical forecasts:   0%|          | 0/365 [00:00<?, ?it/s]
`predict()` was called with `n > output_chunk_length`: using auto-regression to forecast the values after `output_chunk_length` points. The model will access `(n - output_chunk_length)` future values of your `past_covariates` (relative to the first predicted time step). To hide this warning, set `show_warnings=False`.
In [27]:
mape_scores_xgb = mape(actual_series=train, 
                       pred_series=backtest_xgb, intersect=True)

print(f"MAPE scores: {mape_scores_xgb}")

MAPE scores: 1.3317382300483045

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))

backtest_xgb.plot(label="Historical Forecasts")
plt.title("XGBModel: Actual vs Historical Forecasts")
plt.legend()
plt.show()
In [29]:
# Make future predictions 

# TODO: reconcile lag, test and covar
_, future_covariates = covar_series.split_before(pd.Timestamp('2009-11-12'))

future_predictions = model_xgb.predict(n=(df_single.index.max() - test.time_index.min()).days,
                                   past_covariates=future_covariates)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
series.plot(label="Actual")
future_predictions.plot(label="Future Predictions")
plt.title("XGBModel: Future Temperature Predictions")
plt.legend()
plt.show()

`predict()` was called with `n > output_chunk_length`: using auto-regression to forecast the values after `output_chunk_length` points. The model will access `(n - output_chunk_length)` future values of your `past_covariates` (relative to the first predicted time step). To hide this warning, set `show_warnings=False`.

Aggregate monthly averages¶

In [30]:
# Group by station, year, and month, then calculate the mean for temperature and wind
aggregated_df = df.groupby(['station_id', 'year', 'month']).agg({
    'tair': 'mean',
    'tp': 'mean',
    'sf': 'mean'
}).reset_index()

# Combine year and month into a single date column (day=1)
aggregated_df['date'] = pd.to_datetime(aggregated_df[['year', 'month']].assign(day=1))

# Drop the separate year and month columns
aggregated_df = aggregated_df.drop(['year', 'month'], axis=1)
aggregated_df = aggregated_df.sort_values(['station_id', 'date'])

aggregated_df
